title: Hanging Outdoor Bed
summary: A porch-swing-style daybed on a doubled 2x6/2x4 support frame, hung from the ceiling by chain and eye bolts sized to carry a twin mattress and sleeper.
description:
The load path on this bed is the whole design: weight goes from the mattress, to the slats, to the cleats, to the side 2x6s, and only then to the eye bolts — which is exactly why the eye bolts get installed on the longer 2x6 sides and nowhere else. Doubling up the 2x6 frame with a 2x4 cleat underneath isn't decorative bulk, it's what gives that final link in the chain — the eye bolt itself — enough solid wood to bite into.

The end and side trim is genuinely optional structurally, and the plan is upfront about that: the frame alone is strong enough without it. It's there purely to dress up the outside of an otherwise stud-grade frame, which is also why it's fine to upgrade to a nicer species for the trim alone while keeping cheap lumber everywhere it won't show. Whatever hanging hardware you choose has to match your actual ceiling structure — the bolt type is a decision made at the rafters, not at this bed.

tools.note: Predrills for the eye bolts and hanging hardware.

materials.name: Eye bolts, exterior-rated
materials.note: Four for the bed, four for the ceiling — sized to match your actual rafters.

materials.name: Chain, exterior-rated, ~24 ft total
materials.note: 24 ft covers a standard 8 ft ceiling; adjust length to your own ceiling height.

materials.name: Twin mattress
materials.note: Not included in the cost estimate — priced separately based on what you already own or choose to buy.

cutList.note: These carry the eye bolts and the full load of the bed.

steps.title: Cut every part
steps.body: Cut everything before assembly. From the three 8 ft 2x6s, cut two support frame sides 76 1/2" long and two frame ends 37 1/2"; the two sides take a board each and both ends come out of the third. From the two 8 ft 2x4s, cut one 73 1/2" cleat from each. From the six 8 ft 1x3s, cut twelve slats 37 1/2" long, two per board. From the twelve 8 ft 1x2s, cut eight side trims 78" (one per board) and eight end trims 40 1/2" (two per board). Check the arithmetic before cutting: the 37 1/2" ends fitting between the 76 1/2" sides give an outside width of 40 1/2", which is the end trim length, and 76 1/2" plus one 1 1/2" side gives the 78" side trim. Measure your mattress against the 73 1/2" x 37 1/2" platform those numbers produce before you commit the 2x6s.

steps.title: Build the support frame
steps.body: Build the 2x6 frame on a flat surface: stand the two 76 1/2" sides on edge and parallel, then fit the two 37 1/2" ends between them so the frame measures 76 1/2" by 40 1/2" outside. Predrill and drive three 2-1/2" exterior screws through each side into the end grain of each end board, with exterior glue in every joint. Measure both diagonals and adjust until they match before the last corner is tightened; this frame carries the whole bed and a sleeper, and it cannot be squared later. Pick the two straightest, most knot-free 2x6s for the sides, because those are the boards the eye bolts will go into.

steps.title: Attach the cleats
steps.body: Set each 73 1/2" 2x4 cleat against the inside face of a frame side, 3/4" down from the top edge, so the 3/4" slats drop in and finish flush with the top of the 2x6. Predrill and drive 2-1/2" exterior screws with glue through the 2x6 into the cleat, staggering them along its length, then screw from the outside of each frame end into the cleat's end grain. Doubling the 2x6 with the 2x4 this way is not bulk for the look of it: it is what gives the eye bolt enough solid wood to bite into, and it is what carries every pound of load from the slats out to the sides.

steps.title: Add the end trim
steps.body: Attach the 40 1/2" 1x2 end trim to the outside of each frame end, flush at the bottom and flush at both ends with the outside faces of the sides. Four pieces go on each end; stacked, four 1 1/2" boards make 6" against a 5 1/2" 2x6, so the top course stands 1/2" proud and forms the small lip that keeps the mattress from sliding off. Fasten with 1-1/4" exterior finish nails and exterior glue, keeping the courses tight to each other. This trim is purely cosmetic - the frame is plenty strong without it - so it is the one place worth spending on a nicer species while the structural lumber stays cheap.

steps.title: Add the side trim
steps.body: Do the same on the long sides with the eight 78" pieces, four stacked per side, flush at the bottom and running the full 78" so their ends cover the end trim's edges. Four 1 1/2" courses again stack to 6" against a 5 1/2" frame side, so the top course stands 1/2" proud and carries the mattress lip all the way round. Nail and glue as before and keep the courses tight to one another; a gap in the stack reads as a mistake from across the porch.

steps.title: Add the slats and finish
steps.body: Drop the twelve 37 1/2" slats across the cleats and space them evenly along the 73 1/2" cleat run; twelve 2 1/2" slats over that span leaves close to 4" of gap between them. Predrill each slat end, because 1x3 splits readily, and screw both ends down into the cleat with 2-1/2" exterior screws. Sand every surface and every edge, then apply an exterior finish, coating the end grain and the undersides too - this bed lives outdoors and will get rained on regardless of a roof over it.

steps.title: Install the eye bolts
steps.body: Predrill and install the four eye bolts through the doubled 2x6-and-2x4 section at the four corners of the frame, into the long sides only and never into the ends. The load path runs mattress to slats to cleats to sides to eye bolt, so a bolt set in an end board carries nothing the frame was designed to hand it. Drill the pilot straight and sized to the bolt's shank so the threads bite rather than strip, and set each bolt far enough in from the corner to keep full-thickness wood all around it. Check all four sit at the same height, or the bed will hang out of level no matter how you adjust the chain.

steps.title: Hang the bed
steps.body: Rest the finished bed on sawhorses or a table underneath where it will hang, so you never have to take its weight while you work. Locate the ceiling rafters and install four matching eye bolts into them, positioned to line up with the bed's corners; the bolt type is a decision made at the rafters based on what your ceiling structure actually is, and it is the one part of this build worth asking a builder about if you are unsure. Hang the bed with exterior-rated chain from all four corners and adjust the links until a level laid across the frame reads true both ways. Load it gradually, and re-check the level and every piece of hardware after the first week.

images.alt: Hanging outdoor daybed suspended by chains from a porch ceiling
